step.actionDisplay: UP -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> RIGHT -> UP

sections.content:

Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- World model: ACTION7 doesn't work. Orange moves horizontally but not vertically from (34, 38). Need to try vertical movement from other orange positions.
- Plan: Try UP/DOWN from orange at (34, 14) and other positions. Also try combining orange movement with g frame mechanic.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 87 actions; a strong score needs about 81 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The orange square can move DOWN from (34, 14) to (40, 14), (46, 14), (52, 14). It can also move RIGHT from (52, 14) to (52, 20). This means the orange square follows the black path on the board!
Looking at the image, the black path connects:
- Top grid right side -> middle horizontal segment -> down -> left -> bottom grid left side
The orange square can traverse this entire path.
Now I understand the mechanic better. The orange square is a "player" that moves along the black path. Maybe the goal is to move the orange square to a specific position on the path, or to collect greens along the path.
Looking at the current position (52, 20), the orange square is near the bottom grid. The bottom grid has a green at (52, 44).
Let me explore the full path and see if the orange square can reach the bottom grid or interact with the green there.
Actually, looking at the image, the orange square is now at (52, 14) or (52, 20), which is near the bottom-left corner of the path. The bottom grid is at the bottom-right.
Let me try to move the orange square along the path to see if it can reach the bottom grid and interact with the green there.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Orange square follows the black path! It moved DOWN from (34, 14) to (52, 14), then RIGHT to (52, 20). The path connects the top grid to the bottom grid.
Plan: Continue moving orange along the path to explore if it can reach the bottom grid and interact with the green there. Try clicking the green when orange is nearby.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
def get_centers(seg, color):
    nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == color]
    centers = []
    for n in nodes:
        center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])//len(n['boundary']), sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])//len(n['boundary']))
        centers.append(center)
    return centers

# Orange is at (52, 20)
# Try moving RIGHT to reach the bottom grid
seg = current_frame.segmentation
orange = get_centers(seg, 'O')
greens = get_centers(seg, 'N')
print(f"Orange: {orange}, Greens: {greens}")

# Try moving RIGHT
action([{'action': 'RIGHT'}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
orange = get_centers(seg, 'O')
print(f"After RIGHT: {orange}")

# Try RIGHT again
action([{'action': 'RIGHT'}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
orange = get_centers(seg, 'O')
print(f"After RIGHT: {orange}")

# Try RIGHT again
action([{'action': 'RIGHT'}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
orange = get_centers(seg, 'O')
print(f"After RIGHT: {orange}")

# Try RIGHT again
action([{'action': 'RIGHT'}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
orange = get_centers(seg, 'O')
print(f"After RIGHT: {orange}")

# Try RIGHT again
action([{'action': 'RIGHT'}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
orange = get_centers(seg, 'O')
print(f"After RIGHT: {orange}")

# Try RIGHT again
action([{'action': 'RIGHT'}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
orange = get_centers(seg, 'O')
print(f"After RIGHT: {orange}")

# Check if we reached the bottom grid
greens = get_centers(seg, 'N')
print(f"Greens: {greens}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
